title: Thai Lee speaking at the Women in Cloud Summit 2019
text:
I am thrilled to be here I am very
honored to be standing here and I am
very thrilled to be sharing this
wonderful day today with ambitious and
very energetic young women so thank you
very much for inviting me here to be on
stage I would just sit down shortly if
that's okay
so yeah so thank you to the sponsors of
women in cloud Gabriela who is our
executive sponsor for Shi International
and all the amazing women who have
worked so hard to make today a reality
my name is ty Lee I was born to a Korean
parents but because I was born in
Thailand my parents decided to name me
Thai which is not a very typical Korean
name in Korean my name translates to ETA
he which means great peaceful and
freedom and joy which is pretty good
name for a girl in Korea and why freedom
because did you know that Thailand is
one of only Asian countries that was
never invaded by a foreign power so sort
of like Switzerland of Asia so as I was
growing up my father's worked took us
all throughout the world I lived in
Germany I lived in u.s. when I was very
young I lived in Thailand and I lived in
Korea but when I was 15 and I was in
Korea my father decided to ship off his
two eldest daughters myself and my older
sister to us the reason because he felt
that we could have the best education in
the US and best prospects for our lives
in the US so at age 15 I we stayed and
the home of addict actually of my
father's friend in western Massachusetts
in a small town called Amherst
and there are two years later I'm metric
I entered Amherst College because in
1976 Amherst opened its door to women
for the first year the freshman class of
Amherst College had women and that was
1976 so it was very lucky to have gone
to a college then and after I went to
college I went back to Korea to work for
three years the reason I had to save
enough money to get enough tuition to
study in a business goal so three years
later with $25,000 in savings I went off
I came back to Massachusetts and went to
Harvard Business go for two years and
after Harvard I worked for three more
years at Procter & Gamble in Cincinnati
and American Express want to really gain
experience in business and to to save
money to start my own company so during
the three years after getting my MBA I
saved enough twenty thousand dollars to
pay off my second year of tuition at HBS
and had $50,000 in saving and my
starting salary at P&G was only forty
thousand how is this possible I had a
four hundred dollar studio apartment in
Cincinnati Ohio which even back in 1980s
was pretty cheap price for a nice
apartment building so you know I had
worked for many years try to save enough
money to attain my educational goal as
well as getting prepared for business
what I want to share with you today are
some of the steps and decisions that I
took and made in that helped me get to
where I am today so the first decision I
made was the decision to become an
entrepreneur now how and this decision
came when I was merely
17-year old student at Amherst College
now how does and I think back then the
word entrepreneurship was definitely not
in vogue and I had probably never even
heard of the term but I had decided that
with my strengths and weaknesses a lot
of weaknesses in language skills that I
could only be truly successful if I were
to own and run my own company why did I
do that well you know if for those of
you who know Amherst College it's a very
competitive liberal arts college and I
was surrounded at age 17 by very very
ambitious and well-prepared students and
I felt totally inadequate and I thought
that I could never be successful working
for a large corporation because you need
language skills written language
abilities as well as really social
skills that I did not have or could not
develop at the time I do not think that
could be successful being a professor I
didn't think I could be a litigator for
example so I went through a whole list
of career options and ruled to that one
by one and decided that really the only
option for me was to be an entrepreneur
so the first step I took the decision to
become an entrepreneur the second step I
took was to actually be my own best
advocate and it wasn't very easy for
insecure 17-year old girl to be my best
advocate because well I was scared all
really throughout the throughout my
college years and even even later why
was I scared well one I was an outsider
definitely not a typical college student
as I mentioned in Amherst had just gone
co-ed and so throughout the campus there
were only 10% women even though our
class at 30% women so as a woman as a
woman of color there were not very many
minorities this is now going back
to 1970s I'm very old so the world in
seventies was very different place than
it is today so and and definitely not
very many immigrants there were a few
international students mostly from
European countries but definitely not
from Asian countries and you know
shortly after I graduated actually there
were waves of Asian and today you can
hardly walk the campuses of colleges and
university without seeing a lot of
Asians but the world the campus back
then was very different so I felt very
insecure and most of all I did not
really I was not that familiar or
comfortable with American culture so it
was a lot of reasons for me to feel
extremely insecure but I decided that
that would not inhibit me from
cultivating and really crafting the most
ambitious goal possible for myself
so the third decision I made is that as
I look back and assess myself and looked
at my strengths and weaknesses now I
decided the best way for me to achieve
success was to actually outline the
goals for my life and this would be at
KU listicle where I look at all my life
and actually assumed first of all that I
would live to a hundred and actually put
together a template for each decade so
for example my entire twenties was going
to be a preparation to launch this
entrepreneurship so what I did was I
studied as much as I can about business
I read every business magazine cover to
cover I went to business school so that
in case I failed and I knew there was a
large chance that I could feel that I
could go back and get a job so my entire
20s was really learning decade to get me
prepared and I was going to launch a bit
by age 30 which actually Shi was
established in 1989 when I turned 30 and
my 30s was going to be a one of extreme
focus they knew that in order to really
be an entrepreneur you have to be really
committed many of my friends tell me
that they Envy me because I have a lot
of control from my time and that is not
the case because when you're starting
out it takes so much focus and I didn't
want distractions fortunately I was
married by then so that I didn't have to
worry about dating which haha a huge
source of distraction and I didn't start
a family until I hit 39 so I had my
first son at 39 I knew about the
fertility curve that's one of the few
things that really impact women is that
there is set timetable in terms of when
it is best or best chances of securing
children and family so you know all
throughout my 30s I was really
singularly focused on making shi
successful and then at 39 I had my first
son and then a age 44 after my divorce I
chose to be a single mom it was actually
brought harsh to my parents were very
ashamed for many years but actually it
turned out to be a really good decision
for myself
in fact at age 44 I made the record at
New Jersey IVF clinic which which I was
the oldest mom with own eggs so so I
tell you all this
so what I want to share with you is the
methods I use to craft goals so I stated
that each of my decade has goals and
focus that I wanted to put my efforts
energy and time against it
so my 20s was those of learning 30s was
focus on making the startup really
successful 40s was try to incorporate
having family and children into my life
if possible and then 50s I just turned
sixty two months ago so I'm gonna new
thank you I'm gonna new decade and a new
journey but you know my focus in terms
of career and goal is to actually make
sure that Sh I serve that survives
beyond myself so there are really three
holistic aspects to the goal template
that I have I use the first is personal
and family goals so for example my goal
for the last couple of years and
actually hopefully for the next five to
ten years is going to be to try to spend
as much time with my mother who is 84
that means having vacation with her I
even score myself did I really
accomplish this goal I FaceTime with her
every other day at minimum to a point
where she tells me don't call me so much
fortunately it's FaceTime so it's free
my my career and financial goals are
tied together and but most of all you
know it starts with health goal so what
does that mean when I was a Procter &
Gamble you know one of the really
valuable learning ahead is that setting
goals and setting goals you need to make
it smart so specific measurable
actionable relatable time-bound so SMART
goals so for example my health goal
have in a long-term goal which is I
would like to live beyond 100 so that's
a very specific long-term goal I
actually make that into a decade goal so
for me this year I have to make sure
that I get all the annual checkup in
addition to having all the usual metrics
so BMI weight cholesterol I make sure
that if I should have a serious disease
that I could help really maximize my
chances of recovering from it by
discovering it early enough really
without your health there's really
nothing else right that matters in life
so I'm just giving you some examples of
you know how I set the goal something
that you might want to try there there
is a method called gold cultivator which
can go into detail as to how you could
really view your life holistically so as
Steven Covey's says begin with the end
in mind
right you want to know what you want to
accomplish while you're here on earth
because time is short and you know I
can't believe I'm 60 myself but time
will fly so you know the three actions
and decisions I made become an
entrepreneur which I understand many of
you have made already to be your own
best advocate don't beat yourself up
only focus on things that you have
control over don't worry about it rest
of it third is set a goal for your life
cut it into different components they
all work together holistically and work
on it every single day so that at the
end of the day you know where you're
going and for those of you who are
entrepreneurs I have few words of advice
as well first word of that advice I know
many of you might be looking
for external funding don't despair if
you can't find investor in fact one of
the best things that happen to Shi is
that we were not able to get bank credit
line
we weren't even looking for private
equity or venture capital at the time
because I did not believe that our
business would be attractive to that
community so and because we weren't able
to raise money all of our growth over
the last twenty nine years had to be
organic and that gives a company a
certain amount of discipline that cannot
easily be really covered because if
you're growing you take an outside
investor pretty soon you'll find that
you will lose control over not only the
company but but many other aspects of
your business so if you can raise money
great you know there's a lot of benefits
to raising money and accelerating your
business but if you don't it's okay
because maybe like myself you would be
grateful for not being able to raise
money 30 years later second advice I
give is don't don't rush to go public so
I know many of the classmates from
business school and you know their goal
is to raise money grow the company
quickly sell or IPO the company actually
you know there there's a whole lot of
negative that comes from running a
public company so as long as possible as
long as you can try to grow your company
organically and and delay going public
as long as possible so those are the two
words I have for the entrepreneurs who
are on this amazing exciting journey
it's going to be very scary at times but
believe in yourself and most of all it's
okay to fail it is okay to fail
at least you will have tried and you
will have done your best so those are
those are the words from somebody who
has been on this journey for many
decades and hopefully many more decades
to come
so thank you very much thank you time
[Applause]
